title: Fire Truck Loft Bed
summary: A twin loft bed built as an enclosed fire-truck cab — plywood walls on three sides, a ladder side, a windshield side, and a bolt-together frame so it can come apart for moving.
description:
This is a full enclosed loft bed dressed as a fire truck: four distinct plywood wall panels (front cab face, windshield side, ladder side, and an open back for the mattress to load through), each trimmed out separately before the whole thing gets bolted together into one structure. Building each wall as its own flat panel — instead of framing the bed as a box and adding fire-truck details after — is what makes this manageable; you're never fighting gravity or hardware while cutting a window opening or laying out ladder rungs.

Bolt the four walls together rather than screwing them permanently. A loft bed is heavy and awkward through doorways and stairwells; bolted joints mean you can knock it back down to four flat panels to move it, and re-assemble on the other end. Paint the plywood before assembly wherever you can reach it — it's dramatically easier to paint a flat panel on sawhorses than to cut in around installed trim later.
timeLabel: 16–22 hrs across the four wall panels

tools.note: Cuts the windows, doors, wheel, and ladder rungs out of plywood and 1x stock.
Optional — lets you build window/door trim frames separately and attach as one piece.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood or MDF, 4x8 sheet
materials.note: The four wall panels — front, back, and both sides.

materials.name: 1x3 board, 8 ft
materials.note: Wall trim, ladder rungs, and framing — the bulk of the parts count.

materials.name: 2x2 board, 4 ft
materials.note: One for the ladder rails, two more for mattress-support cleats.

materials.name: Carriage bolts, 3" with nuts and washers
materials.note: Bolts the four wall panels together — keep this removable for moving the bed later.

materials.name: Exterior/interior paint, fire-truck red and accent colors

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 30° off square, ends parallel to each other.
Cut a 24" circle and trim off the bottom ~6" so it reads as a wheel behind the cab.

steps.title: Cut and detail the front wall panel
steps.body: Cut the front plywood panel to size and lay out the window and door openings before cutting them with a jigsaw — paint it now if you're painting, while it's flat on sawhorses. Attach the leg trim to the outside edges (nail from the back to hide holes), keeping bottom and outside edges flush, then add the top rails.

steps.title: Trim the front wall and add the wheel and ladder rungs
steps.body: Trim out the window and door openings, then attach the decorative angled 1x3 rungs across the front to read as a ladder, and the cut plywood wheel below them. These pieces are decorative but also tie the front railings together structurally, so don't skip fastening them well.

steps.title: Build the windshield side wall
steps.body: Cut the windshield-side plywood panel and its angled windshield opening. Attach the leg trim (inset 3/4" from the panel edge — a scrap 1x makes a quick spacer gauge) and top rails, then the window sill and mullions.

steps.title: Build the ladder side wall
steps.body: Cut the ladder-side panel and its window opening, attach legs inset the same 3/4" as the other side, and check the ladder support framing is square all the way up before you fasten the ladder rails on. A racked ladder opening is obvious and annoying to fix after the fact.

steps.title: Build the ladder and back wall
steps.body: Build the actual climbing ladder from the two rails and rungs and bolt it to the ladder-side trim. Then build the open back wall — legs, top rails, and sill, with no plywood panel, since this is where the mattress loads in.
materials: Carriage bolts, 3" with nuts and washers

steps.title: Bolt the four walls together
steps.body:
Stand the four wall panels up and bolt them together at the corners with the 3" carriage bolts, washers, and nuts — this is best done in the room the bed will live in, since it's much easier than moving an assembled loft bed through a doorway. Check the whole structure is level and square before tightening every bolt fully.

For heavier loads or a taller loft height, add an extra center leg under the back wall.
materials: Carriage bolts, 3" with nuts and washers

steps.title: Add mattress support and build the bucket
steps.body:
Screw the mattress-support cleats to the inside faces of the side walls (2x2 cleats give more meat to screw slats into than 1x), then rest slats or a bunkie board across them.

Build the bucket last — by this point it's a straightforward small box from the 1x10 sides, 2x2 legs, and plywood bottom. Sand every edge on the whole bed before moving a mattress in.

images.alt: Twin loft bed built and painted to look like a fire truck cab
